coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescent sensing hydrogel platform based on the combination of nanocellulose and S,N-codoped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiz-Palomero, Celia
Soriano, M. Laura
Benítez-Martínez, Sandra
Valcárcel, Miguel
coredata.dc:description: The design of tailor-made gelators has attracted tremendous level of attention for the fabrication of novel nanogels with responsive characteristics. Exploiting the gel properties of nanocellulose (NC) because of its abundance, sustainability and renewability, we reported the fabrication of a fluorescent hydrogel based on S,N-codoped Graphene Quantum Dots (S,N-GQDs) acting as luminophore and sensitizer. The incorporation of the GQDs into the hydrogel resulted in a significant fluorescence intensity enhancement if compared to GQD solutions. In conjunction with recent efforts in the applicability of optical responsive gels, a simple method based on the selectively sensing ability of the GQD/NC hydrogel towards 2,4,5-trichlorophenol (TCP) through the fluorescence enhancement is disclosed. The validity of this strategy is demonstrated with the analysis of TCP in red wine and environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent gel
Nanocellulose
S,N-codoped graphene quantum dots
2,4,5-trichlorophenol
Nanosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of S,N codoped graphene quantum dots
Preparation and characterization of S,N-codoped graphene quantum dots and carboxylated nanocellulose
Selectivity of the proposed method with different chlorophenols
Performance and validation of the proposed method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XPS survey spectrum of S,N-codoped GQDs. (b-d) C 1s, N 1s and S 2p of photoelectron spectra for S,N-codoped GQDs. (e) XPS survey and (f) C 1s photoelectron spectra for c-NC.
TEM images of S,N-codoped GQDs and their size distribution.
(a) SEM image and (b) photograph image under UV light of the GQD/NC hydrogel.
FT-IR spectra of S,N-GQD-based NC hydrogel.

Excitation and emission curves for the S,N-codoped GQDs in aqueous solution and in gel phase at the same concentrations (10wt% of NC, 8mgmL−1 of GQDs). The experiments were performed in the same day and under the same conditions for comparison.
Calibration plot of the quenching of the GQD/NC gel versus TCP. Inset: photoluminescent responses of the gel upon addition of various TCP concentrations, expressed in μgmL−1.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescent sensing hydrogel platform based on the combination of nanocellulose and S,N-codoped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Marie Curie Building, Campus de Rabanales, University of Córdoba, E-14071 Córdoba, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of tailor-made gelators has attracted tremendous level of attention for the fabrication of novel nanogels with responsive characteristics. Exploiting the gel properties of nanocellulose (NC) because of its abundance, sustainability and renewability, we reported the fabrication of a fluorescent hydrogel based on S,N-codoped Graphene Quantum Dots (S,N-GQDs) acting as luminophore and sensitizer. The incorporation of the GQDs into the hydrogel resulted in a significant fluorescence intensity enhancement if compared to GQD solutions. In conjunction with recent efforts in the applicability of optical responsive gels, a simple method based on the selectively sensing ability of the GQD/NC hydrogel towards 2,4,5-trichlorophenol (TCP) through the fluorescence enhancement is disclosed. The validity of this strategy is demonstrated with the analysis of TCP in red wine and environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The flexible three-dimensional network of gels has experienced an explosion of interest in recent decades, especially those gel systems with sensing applications [1]. In the last years, the inclusion of nanomaterials into gels was addressed [2–6]. On the one hand, organogels, [7,8] hydrogels [9] and aerogels [10] were explored as three-dimensional templates offering a simple approach to the spontaneous generation of inorganic nanoparticles (NPs) and organic nanostructures such as nanorods [11]. In other cases, the own NPs were embedded into the gel matrix to improve the mechanical strength of the gels [3] or even to assess any specific stimuli-responsive features for a broad range of applications [12]; in this context, innovative hydrogels were reported based on various low dimensional carbon NPs such as graphene and graphene oxide [13–15] and carbon quantum dots [16] for analytical applications.

On the other hand, NPs can also act as gelators, as this is the case of carboxylated nanocellulose (c-NC) which nanofibers self-assembly spontaneously each other mediated through highly extended hydrogen bonding by virtue of the multitude hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in the cellulosic nanostructure [17,18]. This sustainable nanomaterial derived from many renewable resources has been the subject of a tremendous research effort for future industrial and technological applications (nanocomposites, pharmaceutical crystallization [19], fabrication of lenses [20], inks for 3D (bio)printers [21], biodegradable food storages, nanoreactor [22], etc.) as a virtue of their excellent sorbent capability and chemical reactivity [23]. As NC easily forms hydrogel networks, the combination of NC attributes with the optical properties of a fluorophore or plasmonic NPs led to the formation of gels with exceptional optical features suited for analytical purposes [24–26]. In this regard, the current research investigations focus on the understanding of the fluorescent NP interaction within the cellulosic nanofibrils [27] based on carbon-based fluorescent nanodots [28]. This is the case of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which is in the front of the material science. Recently, the incorporation of heteroatoms into the graphitic nanosheets has implemented considerably some of their outstanding properties. On the one hand, nitrogen doping is preferable used for tuning their electronic properties. On the other hand, the higher polarizable sulphur atoms are of great interest in doping carbon frameworks, as predominantly appears as sulphide bridges CSC, for their advanced applications involving heterogeneous catalysis, sorption and energy conversion/storage [29]. Comparing with singly doping, multiple doped GQDs are receiving great attention mainly due to their versatility as advanced functional materials.

The mixing of NC and GQDs seems to be an excellent strategy resulted in a synergistic enhancement of properties of each individual component, being the mechanical strength of the gel improved and concomitantly enhancement of the GQD fluorescence as occurred with other fluorophore immersed into NC hydrogels [12]. Furthermore, the gel matrix warrants the well dispersion and stabilization of the individual nanodots avoiding aggregation.

Herein, we report for the first time the easy fabrication of a unique tridimensional hydrogels constituting from S,N-GQD nano-islands embedded within NC fibrils. This strategy not only avoids GQD aggregation via π-π stacking of nanosheets but also enhances their luminescence (PL) properties. By virtue of their unique optical properties, the proposed hydrogel displays an excellent sensing ability towards TCP. The method was validated in red wine and water samples, demonstrating its suitability for quick screening of TCP. To our knowledge, fluorescent hydrogels based on NC containing GQDs and their application in sensing has not been reported so far.

Avicel PH-101 cellulose microcrystalline (50μm of particle size), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl radical (98%), sodium hypochlorite solution (10–15%), sodium bromide (>99%), citric acid (>99.5%), fructose (>99%), thiourea (>99%), 2,4,5-trichlorophenol (98%), 2,6-dichlorophenol (98%), 3-chlorophenol (98%), sodium hydroxide pellets (>97%) and citrated gold nanoparticles (diameter size of 10nm) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich; ethanol was purchased from PANREAC.
All reagents were used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water used throughout all experiments was purified through a Millipore system.

Dialysis tubings with 3500 kDA cut off were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Nylon membranes of 0.45-μm pore size were supplied from Sartorius Stedim Biotech (Germany). Oasis HLB SPE tubes were supplied from Waters.
Red wine was purchased from a local supermarket and water samples were collected from Rabanales’s River.

Fluorescence emission and absorption spectra were recorded on a PTI QuantaMaster™ spectrofluorimeter from Photon Technology International equipped with a 75W xenon short arc lamp and a model 814 PMT detection system. Felix 32 software was used to collect and process data. The emission and excitation slit widths were both 2nm.

Infrared (IR) spectra were recorded with a Tensor 27 FT-MIR spectrophotometer equipped with a Hyperion 2000 microscope, using KBr pellets prepared from the samples. Raman characterization of the gel was performed using a DXR Raman microscope with a laser of 780nm working at 10mV.

Images of S,N-GQDs were performed using a FEI Tecnai F30 high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) operated at 300kV, equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analyzer for chemical characterization.
Hydrogel was dried under vacuum for 2days, 3-nm coated with chromium and imaged by scanning electron microscope (SEM) model JEOL JSM 6300, with a typical beam setting of 30kV.
The structural properties of the composite were examined with a Siemens D5000 X-ray diffractometer at a heating rate of 5°Cmin−1 from 25 to 90°C under nitrogen atmosphere.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was used to determine the oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups existing on the surface of nanomaterials. XPS spectra were obtained on a PHOBIOS 150 MCD 5700 spectrometer with non-monochromatic Al Kα radiation and a multi-channel detector. The binding energy scale was referenced by setting the C 1s transition at 284.6eV.
pH of the media was measured with a Basic 20 pH-meter from Crison in all tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of S,N codoped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs codoped were obtained using a slightly modified version of the procedure by Qu et al. [30]. To this end, citric acid (0.42g, 2.19mmol) and thiourea (0.46g, 6.04mmol) were dissolved in 10ml water. The solution was transferred into Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was then sealed and heated at 160°C for 4h in an oven. The final product was adjusted to pH 6 using sodium hydroxide (0.1M) and dialyzed for 6h twice. After the dialysis, the S,N-GQD solution at a concentration of 10mgmL−1 was stored at 4°C in an amber bottle until use.

The preparation of c-NC was reported previously [31] but some modifications were included to up-scale the synthesis, as follows: microcrystalline cellulose (5g), NaBr (12.5mg, 1.21mmol) and TEMPO radical (145mg, 0.08mmol) were suspended in water (375ml) and stirred while a solution of NaOCl (40ml, 648.1mmol) was added dropwise from a syringe remaining pH to 10 with NaOH (1M). The end of the reaction was achieved when no further changes in pH appeared without the need of NaOH. Finally, the reaction was quenched using 5ml of ethanol. The nanomaterial was washed with ultrapure water till pH 7 and centrifuged (at 5000rpm). The nanomaterial was then precipitated using methanol and dried under vacuum. A yield of 92% was obtained.

Gelation experiments were performed at 10wt% of the gelator using c-NC (55mg) in 0.5ml of aqueous solvent containing S,N-GQDs (10mgmL−1). Samples were mixed via vortex and sonication followed by a short centrifuging (for 0.5min at 1300rpm) and heating cycle in a sealed vial for 20s. Transparent gels were observed in few minutes on cooling to ambient temperature after the last centrifugation step to guarantee that all material remains at the bottom of the vial prior gelification. Gel formation was monitored by inversion test.

The as-prepared gels revealed an enhancement of fluorescence, in which the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were 376nm and 439nm. Batch-to-batch reproducibility, in terms of photoluminescent response, was evaluated with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.72%.

The Φ x of S,N-codoped GQDs (30%) dispersed in ultrapure water was measured using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 as the standard (Φ ST
=0.51) using Eq. (1) [32],
(1)
Φ
x
=
Φ
S T
(
g r a
d x
g r a
d
S T
)
(
η x 2
η
S T
2
)
where grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance and η the refractive index of the dispersing media.

The determination of chlorophenolic compounds was performed by fluorescence measurements. An enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of GQDs after the interaction with the target analyte was observed and monitored.

Standard solutions of the TCP were prepared in ultrapure water at different concentrations (range of 0.1–20μgmL−1). For the analysis, aliquots of standards containing different concentration of the analyte were placed in a quartz vial, which was used to form the hydrogels; before gelation, the as-prepared mixture containing the S,N-codoped GQDs and TCP was gently shaken for 30min to ensure complete interaction. The formation of the gel was performed as previously described and the fluorescence signal was recorded exciting at 376nm at room temperature.

To validate the proposed method in real samples, red wine and tap and stream waters were investigated. Samples were spiked with the standard solutions of TCP at two concentration levels (0.25 and 0.5μgmL−1) and were allowed to stand for at least 12h, to ensure the complete interaction with the ingredients of the matrix. After the enrichment step, the homogenized samples were centrifuged (for 10min at 3000rpm) and then filtrated using a nylon filter of 0.45μm to remove the agglomerates and solid residue. Spiked red wines were subjected to solid phase extraction using hydrophilic modified styrene-based polymers as sorbent material for retaining a broad range of interfering acidic and basic components of the matrix.
The resulting solutions were placed in quartz vials in which the hydrogels were formed as described previously. The fluorescence of each hydrogel was then monitored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of S,N-codoped graphene quantum dots and carboxylated nanocellulose
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that heteroatom intercalation in graphitic structures increases the disorder in the nanosheets, being possible the modification of the Fermi level and the carrier density in the graphitic monolayer. Interestingly, whereas nitrogen doped GQDs retain a planar structure, sulphur doping (n-type dopant) protrudes out the planar form of the carbon lattice. Atom-thick GQDs doped with S and N atoms within the sp2-hybridized carbon network is expected to undergo stronger quantum effects derived from monolayer sheets, compared to GQDs or their mono-doping analogues.

The S,N-codoped GQDs were synthesized following a hydrothermal procedure from citric acid and thiourea, obtaining a stable S,N-GQD aqueous solution at neutral pH. The composition of such NPs was characterized by XPS (Fig. 1 ). The XPS survey spectrum of S,N-GQDs (Fig. 1a) exhibits four peaks at 162.9, 284.6, 399.9, 531.3eV, which were attributed to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s XPS (Fig. 1b) shows three peaks at 284.5, 285 and 288.2eV attributed to sp2-hybridized graphitic C (CC), sp3C from CS, CN and CO bonding, and CO from carbonyl and carboxylate groups, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1c) reveals two peaks at 399.6 and 401.2eV associated with pyridinic type (CNC) and pyrrolic type (N(C3)). The high-resolution spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 1d) confirms the presence of C-S-C units with the presence of two peaks at 162.9 (associated to S 2p 3/2) and 164.3eV (associated to S 2p 1/2). The high-resolution O 1s XPS spectrum shows three peaks at 530.9, 532.1 and 534.9eV, which were attributed to oxygen KLL auger, CO and CO species, respectively (Fig. S1) [30,33]. Elemental analysis of the dialyzed S,N-GQDs was performed with EDX for the determination of their S and N composition (see Fig. S2), resulting in carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and sulphur contents of 56.3%, 34.1%, 5.8% and 3.8%, respectively.
The morphology of the graphitic nanodots was also investigated by HR-TEM (Fig. 2 ), observing flat circular shaped nanosheets of an average diameter of 3.52±0.5nm.

The synthesized S,N-GQDs were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 3 . The blue fluorescence of S,N-GQDs could be attributed to the ordered sp2 clusters within the sp3 CO matrix. The maximum fluorescence emission did not exhibit any shift when excited at different wavelengths. Codoped GQDs exhibit their highest fluorescence emission at 439nm when excited at 376nm. The S,N-GQD photoluminescent quantum yield (Φ x ) was of 30%, calculated from Eq. (1). Fig. S3 shows the plot of fluorescence versus absorbance for S,N-GQDs and quinine sulfate, being both obtained with R2 of 0.98.

NC was prepared by oxidation and defibrillation of the microfibers using TEMPO radical. The XPS overview spectrum (Fig. 1e) reveals the presence of carbon and oxygen. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s shows three peaks at 287.4, 288.9 and 289.9eV characteristics of COC, OCO and OCO functionalities, as depicted in Fig. 1f. FT-IR characterization [34] of c-NC confirmed the functionality of the nanocellulose fibers.

c-NC is presented as an efficient gelator due to the interacting surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. This resulted in a strong self-association producing an entanglement of the nanofibers via hydrogen bonding. For that reason, nanocellulose constitutes a compatible gel matrix for hosting a variety of molecules/nanoparticles and even for the detection of different substances.

Our preliminary results in gel formation [24] demonstrated the compatibility of NC hydrogels for hosting chemiluminescent fluorophores in consideration of gel stability and strengthen. The present study aims at the understanding of the interaction between the GQDs containing nitrogen and sulphur dopants and the NC in gel media.

Regarding the gelification process, the purification and physicochemical characteristics of the NPs employed to fabricate the hydrogels are critical parameters. On the one hand, the pH of the media, the concentration of salts, or even the doping characteristics of the carbon-based structures introduced into the NC hydrogel were carefully evaluated for gelification. More specifically, initial attempts to form hydrogels with diverse GQD structures were unsatisfied and in some occasions the gels were rather weak in some cases due to the basicity of the GQD solution or the high concentration of salts. Only those dialyzed graphitic solutions (between 4 and 6h) at neutral pH gave rise to gels.

On the other hand, different types of GQDs with and without doping with nitrogen and sulfur heteroatoms were investigated. In first place, only N-doped GQDs obtained from glycine and citric acid at 200°C [35] did not form hydrogels after purification. GQDs without doping obtained with citric acid at 200°C [36] gave rise to weak gels after 4h of dialyses but of very low fluorescence. The investigated S-doped GQDs were obtained through two different routes; on the one hand, those obtained from fructose and sulfuric acid using an autoclave at 170°C for 4h [37] gave rise to lower fluorescence features than those obtained using the same protocol but at 200°C using citric acid instead fructose; however, after neutralizing and purifying the respective GQDs, low PL were obtained when gels. Lastly, synthesized S,N-GQDs obtained from thiourea and citric acid at 160°C gave rise to high stable gels with excellent PL features, being good candidates for the preparation of strong fluorescent gels with NC fibers. IR characteristic peaks of S,N-GQDs appears at 3170 (υ(OH) and υ(NH)), 1705 (υ (CO)) and 1086 (υ(CS)).

Interestingly, when incorporating the selected PL nanodots in such NC hydrogels, some points should be considered for sensing applications: (i) the loading capacity of the gelator and the fluorophore, (ii) the homogenization, and (iii) long term stability.
Hydrogels obtained using a 9–10wt% of NC were the most stable and strengthen [24]. When incorporating the selected GQDs, a 10wt% of c-NC resulted in the most stable hydrogels.

For a 10wt% of c-NC, gels were formed at different concentration of S,N-GQDs (from 4 to 10mgmL−1) up to 10mgmL−1 of GQDs. At higher concentrations, the graphitic layers induce the formation of favored π-π stacking interactions between the nanosheets starting to destabilize the H-bonded cellulosic network responsible for the gelation. The strongest hydrogel with the highest PL intensity was obtained for 8mgmL−1 of S,N-GQDs in 10wt% of c-NC. Interestingly, to evaluate the stability of the hydrogel with temperature, the diffusion of a metallic sphere (0.5g and 5mm of diameter) into the hydrogel matrix when situated onto their surface while heating at temperatures ranging from 25 to 66°C at a heating rate of 4°Cmin−1 were investigated. Restriction of diffusion in the hydrogels was observed for temperatures below 65°C while hydrogels melted at temperatures above 66°C (gel melting temperature, Tgel ).

Fig. 3a shows a SEM image of the resulted hydrogels revealing an entangled filamentous network. FT-IR spectrum of the hydrogel (Fig. 4 ) mainly exhibited the characteristics of c-NC. A broad absorption band at around 3390cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of OH. The peak at around 2897cm−1 is attributed to the CH symmetric stretching mode of the cellulosic skeleton. The band at 1615cm−1 is assigned to the OH bending mode of absorbed water whereas the typical COH bending mode appeared at around 1413cm−1. As occurred with GQDs, the high PL of the hydrogel made rather difficult their characterization by Raman spectroscopy. Only two peaks at 1096 and 1121cm−1 revealed the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of glycoside bonds, respectively (Fig. S4a).

The X-ray diffraction pattern for the hydrogel is very similar to those obtained with the c-NC, observing the crystalline profile 2θ below 30°C. The sharp (2 0 0) peak observed in Fig. S5 indicated the highly ordered crystalline structure of c-NC [38].

Regarding the inter-nanoparticle interaction in the former gel, it is considered that the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of c-NC are interacting with the oxygen-containing groups of the GQD sheets, via hydrogen bonding. In the later, such interactions produced a significant enhancement of the PL of the GQDs as a result of the lack of graphitic layer aggregation via π-π stacking interaction.

Very bright blue luminescence under the illumination of UV light (365nm) was observed (Fig. 3b). As occurred in dissolution, the maximum emission wavelength of the hydrogel is independent of excitation wavelength. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs is dependent on the environment; thus, the hydrogel exhibits much higher PL intensity than the solutions keeping the same concentration of GQDs, as depicted in Fig. 5 . This dependent PL behavior of GQDs into the embedding gel media reflected the lack of GQD aggregation. In the NC gel, the PL is associated with the interaction between of single sheet of GQDs with the carboxylic groups of NC fibrils which stabilized GQDs and prevented them from aggregation.

The sensing behaviour of the as-prepared hydrogel was investigated with a wide variety of pesticides, from the fungicide chlorophenols to the herbicide atrazine and linuron, typically used in the vid plants. Other recent antimicrobial systems such as metallic nanoparticles are of potential interests for cultivation resistance; thus, gold nanoparticles were also investigated as future contaminant of the grapes. Evaluation of the sensorial behaviour of the hydrogel towards those substances was performed by fluorimetric analysis, resulting in no response except for TCP. TCP belongs to the chlorophenols family which is principally produced in chemical industries, and is considered as potential human carcinogens [39,40], especially those with high degree of chlorination [41]. TCP is usually employed as a fungicide and herbicide and as an intermediate in the manufacture of other pesticide [42–44]. TCP has been found in drinking water being a hazard to human health and the environment. The removal of such hazardous trichlorophenol from the environment is one of the most urgent and critical topics in environmental research [45].

One of the challenges currently confronting the scientific community is the detection with a cost-effective monitoring tool, less expensive than any chromatographic equipment. Thus, we endeavored to evaluate the proposed hydrogel as sensing platform for the fluorescence determination of TCP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the proposed method with different chlorophenols
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of this sensor towards chlorophenols was evaluated using three chlorophenols (mono-, di-, tri-substituted phenols). The enhancement of the signal was studied using 10μgmL−1 of the different derivatives. As depicted in Fig. S6, the higher values of PL intensity were observed for TCP, followed by 2,6-dichlorophenol and finally by 3-chlorophenol. These results evidence the correlation between the enhancement of PL intensity and the substitution of chlorophenols (higher electron-deficient character).

It is expected that the electron-deficient aromatic molecule is directly interacting with the GQD sheets, via π-π stacking interaction, which is surrounded by a NC matrix that regulates the adsorption rates. In the later, such interactions produced a significant increase of the PL intensity of the GQDs which is used as signal to detect and monitor the analyte concentration. Fig. S7 shows a comparison of the fluorescence intensity of the S,N-GQDs and TCP in aqueous solution and in hydrogel state. As can be seen in this figure, a very weak interaction between the S,N-GQDs and the TCP was observed when the experiments were performed in aqueous solution. Interestingly, the hydrogel formation led to the stabilization of the analyte interaction with the graphitic nanostructure, in other words, there is a reinforcement of the non-covalent interaction between the aromatic analyte and the non-aggregated tiny carbon nanosheets possibly due to π-π stacking interaction and, therefore, a strongly fluorescence emission was obtained. This property allows the exploitation of the sensing behaviour of PL hydrogels versus TCP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance and validation of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: The potential of such PL hydrogels for determining TCP was evaluated by the PL changes in presence of the analyte to obtain the calibration curve of the method. The method was characterized on the basis of its linearity, sensibility, precision and extraction efficiency. A linear response of the analytical signal -relative fluorescence response (I0/I)- versus increasing concentrations of TCP was obtained in the range of 0.1–20μgmL−1 (Fig. 6 ). The detection (LOD) and quantification (LQ) limits were determined to evaluate the sensitivity of the proposed method. LOD, calculated as three times the standard deviation (SD) of the blank signal divided by the slope of the calibration curve, was of 0.07μgmL−1. The LQ, established as 10 times the standard deviation of the blank signal divided by the slope of the calibration curve, was of 0.22μgmL−1.

For evaluating the precision of the analytical procedure, repeatability and reproducibility were investigated by quintuplicate measurements, and expressed in terms of RSD. The repeatability was found to be 0.77%, while the reproducibility in different days was of 1.39%.

The above results demonstrated the great potential of the PL hydrogels as sensing analytical tools for monitoring TCP. Moreover, the practicability of the method was applied in tap water, stream water and red wine samples. Since no positive samples were found, enriched samples at two concentration levels of the analyte (0.25 and 0.5μgmL−1) were analyzed in quintuplicate using the previously described procedure. In all cases, no matrix effect was observed. The obtained recoveries and RSD values are illustrated in Table S1, demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method for monitoring of TCP with a low-cost equipment.
The combination of NC hydrogel with GQDs provides superior analytical tools with high sensitivity and great potential in sensing applications.

The GQD/NC hydrogel exhibits an excellent batch-to-batch reproducibility and an enhancement of the fluorescence signal. This fluorescence system offers a convenient handling in comparison to other sensing probes in solution; this is mainly due to the stabilization of the graphene nanoparticles and the specific interactions with the analyte in the gel media. Thus, the novel GQD/NC hydrogel was applied for the determination of specific trichlorophenol in red wine and water samples, demonstrating the applicability of the novel sensing gel system. The principle of sensing lies on the ability of the graphene nanosheets to interact with the aromatic ring of the TCP.

According to the great potential of GQD/NC hydrogels, this work could illustrate future paths for designing biomedical strategies in sensing for a quick screening or even in regenerative medicine as printable conductive inks [46].

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude to the Spanish Ministry of Innovation and Science for funding Project CTQ2014-52939R. We also want to thank the Central Service of Research Support, SCAI (UCO), and the Institute of Fine Chemistry and Nanochemistry (UCO) for the microscopic and spectroscopic equipments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.02.006.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gels with sense: supramolecular materials that respond to heat, light and sound
Immobilization of phosphorescent quantum dots in a sol–gel matrix for acetone sensing
Composites of graphene and other nanocarbons with organogelators assembled through supramolecular Interactions
Highly fluorescent xerogels with entrapped carbon dots for organic scintillators
Synthesis of gold nanoparticles within a supramolecular gel-phase network
New functional materials based on self-assembling organogels: from serendipity towards design
Hydrogel networks as nanoreactors: a novel approach to silver nanoparticles for antibacterial applications
Making flexible magnetic aerogels and stiff magnetic nanopaper using cellulose nanofibrils as templates
Supramolecular gel-assisted formation of fullerene nanorods
Nanoparticle–hydrogel composites: concept, design, and applications of these promising, multi-functional materials
Short peptide based hydrogels: incorporation of graphene into the hydrogel
Alginate–graphene oxide hybrid gel beads: an efficient copper adsorbent material
Toward 3D graphene oxide gels based adsorbents for high-efficient water treatment via the promotion of biopolymers
Fluorescent carbon quantum dot hydrogels for direct determination of silver ions
Cellulose-based hydrogels: present status and application prospects
A click-chemistry approach to cellulose-based hydrogels

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: D.J. Levinson, T. Glonek, Microbial cellulose contact lens, Patent. US 7832857 B2.

pH-Triggered molecular alignment for reproducible SERS detection via an AuNP/Nanocellulose Platform
Modification of cellulose nanofibrils with luminescent carbon dots
Semiconductor and carbon-based fluorescent nanodots: the need for consistency
Sulfur-doped porous carbons: synthesis and applications
Highly luminescent S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots with broad visible absorption bands for visible light photocatalysts
Homogeneous suspensions of individualized microfibrils from tempo-catalyzed oxidation of native cellulose
Relative fluorescence quantum yields using a computer-controlled luminescence spectrometer
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Ternary composites of nanocellulose, carbonanotubes and ionic liquids as new extractants for direct immersion single drop microextraction
Determination of TiO2 nanoparticles in sunscreen using N-doped graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe
Graphene quantum dots as sensor for phenols in olive oil
Sulphur doping: a facile approach to tune the electronic structure and optical properties of graphene quantum dots
An improved X-ray diffraction method for cellulose crystallinity measurement
Adsorption of 2,4,5-trichlorophenol by organo-montmorillonites from aqueous solutions: kinetics and equilibrium studies
Ambient Water Quality for Chlorinated Phenols

sb:author.ce:surname: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hazardous Substances Data Bank (HSDB, Online Database)

sb:publisher.sb:name: National Toxicology Information Program, National Library of Medicine

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: IARC Monographs on the Evaluation of the Carcinogenic Risk of Chemicals to Humans: Some Halogenated Hydrocarbons. International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC). Volume 20. World Health Organization, Lyon. 1979.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The Merck Index. An Encyclopedia of Chemicals, Drugs, and Biologicals
Interaction of copper and 2,4,5-trichlorophenol on bioremediation potential and biochemical properties in co-contaminated soil incubated with Clitocybe maxima
A 3D bioprinting system to produce human-scale tissue constructs with structural integrity

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Celia Ruiz-Palomero finished her Master’s thesis on 2011 under the supervision of Dr. M.L. Soriano and prof. M. Valcárcel in the University of Córdoba. She received the Ph.D degree on December of 2016 in the analytical department of the same university. Her research line focused on the applications of Nanocellulose for analytical purposes. She also worked in the crystallization of novel isomorphic drugs into novel organogels in Durham University with Professor Jonathan Steed.

M. Laura Soriano received her B.Sc. and Ph.D. degrees from the Universidad de Castilla La-Mancha (UCLM, Spain) in 2003 and 2007, respectively. She worked in the chemistry department at Durham University with Professor Jonathan Steed (England 2007–2009) and in the pharmaceutical department at the University of Trieste with Professor Maurizio Prato (Italy, 2009–2011) as Postdoctoral Fellows. In 2012, she joined the analytical department at Córdoba University with Professor Miguel Valcárcel. Her actual research interests lie on the functionalization, characterization and separation of a variety of nanostructures and gel systems for analytical and pharmaceutical purposes.

Sandra Benítez-Martínez obtained the Environmental Sciences degree in 2007 at the University of Córdoba. In order to expand her scientific knowledge, she attended a scientific Post degree Master in Advanced Fine Chemistry carrying out a Master Project focused in using graphene nanosheets as pseudostationary phase in electrokinetic chromatography (2011). Later, she started her Doctorate studies focused in the use of graphene and graphene derivatives as tools for the improvement of the analytical process and received her Ph.D degree in 2015. Nowadays she has a post-doctoral fellow in the Analytical Chemistry Department of the University of Córdoba and her actual research interests lie in the study about nano-enzymatic systems.

Miguel Valcárcel has been Full Professor of Analytical Chemistry at the University of Córdoba since 1976 being his master research lines Analytical Nanoscience and Nanotecnology and automatization/miniaturization/quality of (bio)chemical measurement processes. He is the author and co-author of more than 900 articles indexed in international databases (Hirsch index: 53), nine monographs, ten textbooks and twenty chapters of multi-authors books. He has been the coordinator of 25 Spanish scientific research projects and 14 of international nature, and of 12 contracts with private firms, and has promoted a spin-off devoted to nanotechnology. Professor Valcárcel has been supervisor/co-supervisor of 75 doctoral theses and invited lecturer at 80 international meetings. He is the recipient of scientific national awards (ex. National Award in Chemistry in Spain, 2005) and international awards (ex. DAC-EuCheMS Award 2015) prizes as well as Doctor Honoris Causa by the University of Valencia (2011 Spain) and full member of the Spanish Royal Academy of Sciences.
